rationale: The page contains significant policy information regarding tribal governance and forest rights. It details the Adi Karmayogi Abhiyan, its objectives, and the three pillars of leadership. It also covers the Forest Rights Act (FRA) of 2006, including its nodal agency, eligibility criteria for rights holders, and the specific types of rights granted under the act.
cleanedText:
Key Features of Tribal Village Vision 2030 Declaration
* Village-level planning: Actionable goals in education, health, livelihoods, social & financial inclusion, and infrastructure.
* Institutional mechanism: Adi Sewa Kendras in every village as single-window service centres.
* Adi Sewa Samay: Villagers contribute 1 hour/week of voluntary service.
About Adi Karmayogi Abhiyan
It is a national movement to build a decentralized tribal leadership and governance ecosystem.
* Coverage: It marks the world’s largest tribal grassroots leadership programme, covering 11 crore citizens in 30 States and UTs.
* Key Objectives:
* Train and mobilize 20 Lakh tribal changemakers across 1 Lakh villages in 550 tribal-dominated districts.
* Facilitate Village Vision 2030 Planning & Development Action Plan.
* Promote last-mile convergence and effective service delivery.
* It forms a key part of the Janjatiya Gaurav Varsh (from 15 November 2024 to 15 November 2025) to Commemorate Bhagwan Birsa Munda's 150th birthday.
* Multi-Department Convergence: Tribal Welfare, Rural Development, Women & Child Development, Jal Shakti, School Education, and Forest.
Three Pillars of Leadership
* Aadi Karmayogi: Government officers (State to Village level).
* Aadi Sahayogi: Youth leaders, teachers, doctors, social workers.
* Aadi Saathi: SHG members, tribal leaders in volunteers, cultural icons.
8.7.2. FOREST RIGHTS ACT, 2006
Why in the News?
Scheduled Tribes and Other Traditional Forest Dwellers (Recognition of Forest Rights) Act, 2006 or Forest Rights Act (FRA) has completed 20 years in 2026.
About FRA
* Objective: To recognize the rights of forest-dwelling communities who have resided on forest lands for generations, ensuring sustainable livelihoods and ecological security.
* Nodal Implementation Agency: Ministry of Tribal Affairs (MoTA) along with Tribal Departments at State Level.
* Rights Holders: Forest dwelling Scheduled Tribes (STs) and Other Traditional Forest Dwellers (OTFDs).
* OTFD means any member or community who has for at least three generations prior to December 13, 2005 primarily resided in and who depend on the forest or forests land for livelihood needs.
Rights Under Forest Rights Act (FRA)
The Act Grants:
* LAND: Rights over forest land (up to 4 hectares).
* LIVE: Rights of Self-Cultivation and Habitation for livelihood.
* MFP: Rights to collect minor forest produce.
* GRAZE: Grazing rights for livestock.
* MANAGE: Rights to protect and manage forests.
* MORE: Other community rights.
All Rights Are:
* HERITABLE but not transferable or sellable.
* JOINTLY REGISTERED in names of both spouses (if married).
* EXEMPT from prior clearances under Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980.
Empowering Forest Dwellers and Traditional Communities
